critic_raw:
YNet's trajectory forecasting methodology presents several methodological concerns.

1. Segmentation maps are used as environmental context, but the segmentation maps themselves are generated by a separate segmentation model (not the ground-truth semantic labels). The accuracy of trajectory prediction is therefore coupled to the accuracy of the segmentation model in addition to the trajectory model's own quality. The paper does not report how performance degrades when segmentation quality is lower (e.g., in occluded or cluttered scenes), which is precisely the condition where trajectory prediction is hardest and most needed.

2. The Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) and ETH/UCY datasets capture pedestrian motion in controlled or limited environments. The trajectory distributions in these datasets are heavily dominated by straight-line walking with limited multi-modality. The YNet model claims to handle aleatoric uncertainty (environmental unpredictability), but if the evaluation datasets have low trajectory diversity, the multi-modal distribution claimed by the model may not be meaningfully tested. High performance on these benchmarks may reflect fitting the dominant straight-line mode rather than genuine multi-modal distribution coverage.

3. The two-stage prediction (goals then waypoints then remaining trajectory) introduces a cascading error structure: errors in goal prediction propagate to waypoint sampling, which propagate to final trajectory prediction. The paper does not report an oracle experiment where the true goal is provided at test time, which would decompose how much of the final error is attributable to goal prediction versus trajectory completion. Without this decomposition, it is impossible to assess whether the waypoint mechanism is genuinely helping or whether the model would perform comparably with a single-stage architecture.

4. The U-Net-based encoding architecture processes trajectory heatmaps at a fixed spatial resolution. For the SDD dataset which covers large outdoor areas via drone footage, the resolution determines how fine-grained the goal distribution can be. If the resolution is too coarse, goals in neighboring spatial bins will be conflated, inflating apparent goal prediction accuracy without corresponding improvement in actual trajectory precision.
all_issues_raised: Segmentation maps from an external model couple trajectory performance to segmentation accuracy; degradation under poor segmentation is unreported
Evaluation datasets (SDD, ETH/UCY) have low trajectory diversity dominated by straight-line motion; multi-modal distribution coverage is not meaningfully tested
Cascading goal-waypoint-trajectory error structure is not decomposed via oracle experiments; contribution of waypoint mechanism is unclear
Fixed spatial resolution of U-Net heatmap encoding may conflate nearby goals on large-scale datasets, inflating apparent goal prediction accuracy
Two-stage goal-then-path sampling assumes conditional independence not enforced in multi-agent scenarios
Static segmentation maps cannot represent dynamic environmental changes; aleatoric factor claim is overstated
minADE/minFDE metrics reward high-variance sampling without measuring calibration or distributional accuracy
U-Net skip connections' contribution to performance not ablated against simpler encoder-decoder
Dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning not controlled when comparing across SDD and ETH/UCY
Best-of-K (minADE/minFDE) evaluation rewards models that produce diverse but partially degenerate samples; no diversity penalty or full-distribution metric reported
Segmentation map contribution cannot be disentangled from U-Net architecture or waypoint conditioning without an ablation using trajectory history only
SDD results not reported per agent type; aggregate ADE/FDE hides systematic failure on minority agent classes with different dynamics
Two-stage procedure compounds errors from waypoint distribution miscalibration; waypoint calibration is not evaluated
ETH/UCY benchmark split not validated against known data leakage issues in certain leave-one-scene-out protocols
